messages.author: SKitter_
messages.text: variants/rak4631/variant.h line 109-ish PWRMGT_VOLTAGE_BOOTLOCK I also adjusted the PWRMGT_LPCOMP_REFSEL(line 113) to 2 to compensate for the lower voltage. (edited)
messages.content_html: variants/rak4631/variant.h line 109-ish PWRMGT_VOLTAGE_BOOTLOCK I also adjusted the PWRMGT_LPCOMP_REFSEL(line 113) to 2 to compensate for the lower voltage. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: SKitter_
messages.text: To be honest, I’m trying to backtrace the hardware now. It was the LTO pack from voltaic enclosures. It was my first soldering project and I haven’t touched it or even seen it in 6+ months since I set the node up, so I’m trying to determine how the voltage is set up because there’s a couple different configurations and voltages that it supports. (edited)
messages.content_html: To be honest, I’m trying to backtrace the hardware now. It was the LTO pack from voltaic enclosures. It was my first soldering project and I haven’t touched it or even seen it in 6+ months since I set the node up, so I’m trying to determine how the voltage is set up because there’s a couple different configurations and voltages that it supports. (edited)
messages.reply_html:
WZNRXSN
I think I understand now. Doing this is not a problem, but in the future, the code needs to be recompiled to use the LTO battery. However, I am a bit confused about the fact that the maximum operating voltage of the LTO battery is 2.8V. Are you using a boost chip? The value of PWRMGT_LPCOMP_REFSEL is compared based on the module's VDD, which is much higher than 2.8V.
